network.overall_strategy: This network models 10 convergent prebiotic pathways to glycine across three environments. Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation on iron-sulfur minerals generates formate and formaldehyde as foundational C1 building blocks. Fischer-Tropsch chemistry provides an alternative route to formaldehyde via CO. These C1 feedstocks enter Surface chemistry where Strecker synthesis, cyanosulfidic reactions, and the formose reaction generate key hub intermediates: aminoacetonitrile, glyconitrile, glycolic acid, and glyoxylate. Multiple convergent routes—Strecker hydrolysis, amination of glycolic acid, reductive amination of glyoxylate, hydantoin hydrolysis, and UV-driven methylamine carboxylation—all terminate at glycine. Literature-guided conditions, mineral catalysts, and carbon atom tracing are incorporated throughout.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, H2-rich fluid

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed on the greigite (Fe₃S₄) surface, which mimics the active site of [Fe₄S₄] clusters in carbon monoxide dehydrogenase. Surface-bound H₂ dissociates and reduces CO₂ via a two-electron transfer to produce formate (HCOO⁻).
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate has been demonstrated as an analog of the acetyl-CoA pathway. Hydrothermal serpentinization provides the H₂ driving force (Preiner et al., 2020; Herschy et al., 2014). This is the foundational carbon fixation step.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: Subseafloor hydrothermal, reducing conditions

reactions.mechanism: Formate is further reduced on magnetite surfaces. H₂ dissociates on the Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ surface, transferring hydride to formate to produce formaldehyde (HCHO) with loss of water.
reactions.reasoning: Magnetite-catalyzed stepwise reduction of CO₂ through formate to formaldehyde is thermodynamically favorable under hydrothermal conditions where H₂ partial pressures are high due to serpentinization (Ménez et al., 2018). Formaldehyde is the most important C1 building block in the network.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift on pyrite

conditions.other: Black smoker vent conditions

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O via the reverse water-gas shift reaction on pyrite surfaces. The FeS₂ surface facilitates electron transfer and CO₂ activation at elevated temperatures.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction produces CO under black smoker conditions. Pyrite surfaces catalyze this reaction at elevated temperatures (Wächtershäuser, 1988; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997). CO is a critical feedstock for Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch type synthesis of formaldehyde

conditions.other: Gas-phase or supercritical conditions at vent interface

reactions.mechanism: CO is hydrogenated on magnetite surfaces in a Fischer-Tropsch-type reaction. Chemisorbed CO reacts with dissociated H₂ to form surface-bound formyl species, which are further hydrogenated to formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch type catalysis on magnetite produces C1 oxygenates including formaldehyde and methanol from CO + H₂ (McCollom et al., 1999). This provides an alternative hydrothermal route to formaldehyde independent of formate. FTT synthesis has been documented to produce α-amino acids (Sources 1790).

reactions.name: Formaldehyde-ammonia condensation to aminomethanol

conditions.other: Aqueous, alkaline pH 8-10, evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of NH₃ to the carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde produces the carbinolamine intermediate aminomethanol (NH₂CH₂OH). On clay surfaces, the reactants are concentrated by adsorption, increasing effective local concentrations.
reactions.reasoning: Aminomethanol (NH₂CH₂OH) has been experimentally identified as a key Strecker intermediate (Sources 10878). The condensation of formaldehyde and ammonia is rapid and spontaneous in aqueous solution. Clay minerals concentrate reactants and stabilize intermediates (Sources 12438, 13449).

reactions.name: Strecker nucleophilic addition of HCN to aminomethanol

conditions.other: Near-neutral to alkaline pH, aqueous

reactions.mechanism: Aminomethanol dehydrates to form the imine (methanimine, CH₂=NH), which then undergoes nucleophilic addition of CN⁻ to produce aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN). The clay surface templates the reaction and excludes water from the active site.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis proceeds through imine formation and cyanide addition. Aminonitriles are well-established intermediates in the pathway from aldehydes to amino acids (Sources 6942, 11365, 10835, 21189). Cyanohydrin and aminonitrile formation is documented in meteorite parent body aqueous alteration (Sources 10834, 6705).

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: Aqueous, mildly acidic to neutral pH, prebiotic pool

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (-C≡N) of aminoacetonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first to the amide (glycinamide, NH₂CH₂CONH₂), then further hydrolysis of the amide to the carboxylic acid, yielding glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH) and releasing NH₃.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is the final step of the Strecker synthesis. Although energy barriers exist for this step (Sources 10797), hydrolysis proceeds under mildly heated aqueous conditions. Hydrolysis has been confirmed in meteorite parent body conditions (Sources 10835, 6957, 15642, 16363).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic glyconitrile formation

conditions.other: UV irradiation, presence of H₂S, cyanosulfidic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with HCN via nucleophilic addition of CN⁻ to the carbonyl carbon, producing glycolonitrile (HOCH₂CN, glyconitrile). Under cyanosulfidic conditions, H₂S and UV light may activate HCN and promote the reaction.
reactions.reasoning: Glyconitrile formation from formaldehyde and HCN is the entry point to cyanosulfidic protometabolism leading to amino acids (Sources 9375, 9376). The reaction is rapid in aqueous solution. Cyanosulfidic chemistry driven by HCN and H₂S produces precursors to modern biomolecules (Sources 21196, 21195).

reactions.name: Glyconitrile ammonolysis to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: Alkaline pH, aqueous pool

reactions.mechanism: The hydroxyl group of glyconitrile (HOCH₂CN) is displaced by ammonia in an SN2-type reaction, or glyconitrile equilibrates through the cyanohydrin pathway with aminonitrile. Under alkaline conditions, NH₃ substitutes for OH to yield aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanohydrins and aminonitriles are in equilibrium in the presence of ammonia; the irreversible hydrolysis of aminonitriles drives formation of amino acids (Sources 9381, 10835). This connects the cyanosulfidic glyconitrile pathway to the Strecker aminoacetonitrile intermediate.

reactions.name: Formose aldol condensation to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline conditions, warm evaporitic pool with borate

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo aldol condensation catalyzed by Ca(OH)₂ or borate minerals. The first formaldehyde is converted to the enolate (via Cannizzaro-type activation), which attacks the second formaldehyde to yield glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO). Borate minerals complex with and stabilize the product.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction is the primary abiotic route to sugars from formaldehyde. Glycolaldehyde is the first product and a key C2 intermediate. Borate minerals stabilize sugars and prevent degradation (Sources 13497, 13483, 13485, 13490). This is critical for connecting C1 chemistry to C2 intermediates.

reactions.name: Glycolaldehyde oxidation to glycolic acid

conditions.other: UV irradiation, shallow pool, anoxic but with oxidants from UV photolysis of water

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde undergoes UV-promoted oxidation of its aldehyde group to a carboxylic acid. TiO₂ photocatalysis generates reactive oxygen species from water, which oxidize the aldehyde to glycolic acid (HOCH₂COOH).
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde polymerization via the formose reaction produces glycolaldehyde, which is further oxidized to glycolic acid as documented in hydrothermal experiments (Sources 2608, 11370). This is a key step converting sugar aldehydes to hydroxy acids, bridging formose chemistry to amino acid precursors.

reactions.name: Glycolic acid amination to glycine

conditions.other: Slightly basic pH 8.3-10.5, hydrothermal conditions

reactions.mechanism: Glycolic acid is aminated by replacement of the α-hydroxyl group with an amino group. The FeS/Fe(OH)₂ surface activates the C-OH bond and facilitates nucleophilic substitution by NH₃ to produce glycine. This is a reductive amination proceeding through an imine intermediate on the mineral surface.
reactions.reasoning: FeS and Fe(OH)₂ catalyze amino acid synthesis from hydroxy acid precursors and ammonia under mildly basic aqueous conditions (Sources 1025). Glycolic acid is a documented key intermediate in glycine synthesis from formaldehyde (Sources 2608, 11370). Al-rich smectites also enhance this conversion (Sources 13449, 13450).

reactions.name: Glycolic acid oxidation to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV irradiation, anoxic surface pool

reactions.mechanism: The α-hydroxyl group of glycolic acid is oxidized to a carbonyl, converting the alcohol to an aldehyde/ketone and yielding glyoxylate (OHCCOOH/OCHCOO⁻). TiO₂ photocatalysis provides the oxidizing power under UV irradiation.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is a central prebiotic intermediate formed by oxidation of glycolic acid. It plays a critical role in protometabolic networks overlapping with the TCA and glyoxylate cycles (Sources 995, 20732, 23036). Its formation from glycolic acid under UV conditions is thermodynamically favorable.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: pH ~7-9, with nitrogen source (NH3 or hydroxylamine)

reactions.mechanism: Glyoxylate undergoes reductive amination: NH₃ attacks the aldehyde carbonyl of glyoxylate to form an imine, which is then reduced by Fe⁰ acting as an electron donor to yield glycine. The Fe⁰/FeS surface provides both the reducing equivalents and the catalytic surface.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids by Fe⁰ and nitrogen sources (ammonia, hydroxylamine) is documented as a plausible prebiotic route to amino acids (Sources 9396). Glyoxylate is the simplest α-keto acid and directly produces glycine upon transamination (Sources 23061, 9400). This pathway mirrors biological transaminase reactions.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal hydantoin formation

conditions.other: Subcritical hydrothermal, prolonged heating

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with HCN and NH₃ to form aminoacetonitrile, which cyclizes with another CO₂/formaldehyde unit under prolonged hydrothermal conditions to form hydantoin (imidazolidine-2,4-dione). The pyrite surface catalyzes the cyclization by adsorbing and orientating the intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin is a documented hydrothermal product formed from formaldehyde, HCN, and NH₃ under prolonged heating. Hydrolysis of hydantoin releases glycine, as confirmed experimentally (Sources 22118, 22849). This represents a 'storage' mechanism for glycine precursors under harsh hydrothermal conditions.

reactions.name: Hydantoin hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: Warm prebiotic pool, neutral pH, prolonged aqueous exposure

reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin undergoes ring-opening hydrolysis in two steps: first the 4-position amide bond is cleaved to produce N-carbamoylglycine, which is then further hydrolyzed to glycine, CO₂, and NH₃.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of hydantoin significantly increases glycine yields, as confirmed in hydrothermal experiments (Sources 22118, 22849). This pathway allows glycine to be generated from a relatively stable cyclic precursor, providing a temporal buffer between harsh hydrothermal synthesis and milder surface conditions.

reactions.name: Glyconitrile hydrolysis to glycolic acid

conditions.other: Aqueous, mildly acidic to neutral pH

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of glyconitrile (HOCH₂CN) undergoes hydrolysis in two steps: first to the amide (glycolamide, HOCH₂CONH₂), then further hydrolysis to glycolic acid (HOCH₂COOH) with release of NH₃.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanohydrin hydrolysis to hydroxy acids is a well-documented aqueous reaction. Glyconitrile hydrolysis provides a direct pathway from the cyanosulfidic intermediate to glycolic acid, connecting HCN-based and formose-based chemistry (Sources 9381, 2618). This creates a convergence point at glycolic acid.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of formaldehyde to methylamine

conditions.other: Reducing hydrothermal conditions

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with NH₃ to form the imine (methanimine, CH₂=NH), which is reduced by H₂ on magnetite surfaces to yield methylamine (CH₃NH₂). The mineral surface facilitates H₂ dissociation and hydride transfer.
reactions.reasoning: Methylamine has been detected alongside glycine in comet 67P (Sources 5907, 1263) and is a plausible hydrothermal reduction product. Reductive amination of aldehydes on mineral surfaces is well-documented. Methylamine serves as a precursor for the UV-driven glycine synthesis pathway.

reactions.name: UV-driven carboxylation of methylamine to glycine

conditions.other: UV irradiation (<200 nm), no ozone layer, surface tidal flat

reactions.mechanism: Under UV irradiation, methylamine is activated (C-H bond homolysis generates ·CH₂NH₂ radical), which reacts with CO₂ (or CO₂·⁻ radical anion generated on TiO₂) to form glycine through radical carboxylation.
reactions.reasoning: UV photochemical synthesis of glycine from methylamine and CO₂ has been demonstrated in laboratory experiments simulating prebiotic and interstellar conditions (Sources 10826). Methylamine and CO₂ are both abundant prebiotic feedstocks. TiO₂ photocatalysis on early Earth surfaces could have driven this reaction.

reactions.name: Strecker cyanide addition: aminomethanol + HCN → aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: Near-neutral to alkaline pH, aqueous

reactions.mechanism: Aminomethanol dehydrates to form the imine (methanimine, CH₂=NH), which then undergoes nucleophilic addition of CN⁻ to the electrophilic carbon, producing aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN). Clay surfaces template the reaction geometry.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis proceeds through imine formation and cyanide addition. Aminonitriles are well-established intermediates (Sources 6942, 11365, 10835, 21189). This step connects the aminomethanol intermediate to the aminoacetonitrile hub.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation → Strecker synthesis of glycine
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formate then formaldehyde in hydrothermal vents. Formaldehyde reacts with NH₃ and HCN via the classical Strecker pathway at the surface to produce aminoacetonitrile, which is hydrolyzed to glycine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch → Strecker synthesis of glycine
pathways.description: CO₂ is converted to CO via reverse water-gas shift, then CO is hydrogenated to formaldehyde via Fischer-Tropsch chemistry. Formaldehyde enters the Strecker pathway at the surface.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glyconitrile → aminoacetonitrile → glycine
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and HCN react under cyanosulfidic conditions to form glyconitrile, which is converted to aminoacetonitrile by ammonia, then hydrolyzed to glycine.

pathways.name: Formose → glycolic acid amination to glycine
pathways.description: Formaldehyde undergoes formose aldol condensation to glycolaldehyde, which is oxidized to glycolic acid. Glycolic acid is aminated to glycine on FeS/Fe(OH)₂ surfaces.

pathways.name: Formose → glyoxylate reductive amination to glycine
pathways.description: Formaldehyde → glycolaldehyde → glycolic acid → glyoxylate. Glyoxylate undergoes reductive amination with Fe⁰ to yield glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glyconitrile → glycolic acid → glycine (amination)
pathways.description: Glyconitrile from cyanosulfidic chemistry is hydrolyzed to glycolic acid, which is then aminated to glycine under hydrothermal conditions.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glyconitrile → glycolic acid → glyoxylate → glycine
pathways.description: Glyconitrile is hydrolyzed to glycolic acid, oxidized to glyoxylate, then reductively aminated to glycine. This merges cyanosulfidic and metabolic cycle chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal hydantoin pathway
pathways.description: Formaldehyde, HCN, and NH₃ cyclize under prolonged hydrothermal conditions to form hydantoin, a stable glycine precursor that releases glycine upon hydrolysis in cooler pools.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch → formose → glyoxylate → glycine
pathways.description: CO is converted to formaldehyde via FTT, enters the formose reaction to glycolaldehyde, then glycolic acid, glyoxylate, and finally glycine via reductive amination.

pathways.name: UV-driven methylamine carboxylation to glycine
pathways.description: Formaldehyde is reductively aminated to methylamine under hydrothermal conditions. Methylamine is then photocarboxylated with CO₂ under UV irradiation at the surface to produce glycine directly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Textbook Strecker pathway with strong literature support. Environmental flow Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical is ideal. Carbon tracing matches literature: formaldehyde supplies α-carbon, HCN supplies carboxyl carbon. Formate-to-formaldehyde step (rxn_002) on magnetite is the weakest link but plausible. Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis energy barrier noted in literature is acknowledged.
suggestions: Consider specifying pH and temperature for rxn_002 formate reduction; literature suggests this step has low yields without strong reducing conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Fischer-Tropsch variant of P1. RWGS on pyrite and FTT formaldehyde synthesis are both experimentally established. Literature on FTT catalysis for amino acid precursors supports this route. Same high-quality Strecker finish as P1.
suggestions: Specify FTT catalyst mineral (e.g., iron-nickel alloy or magnetite) for rxn_004 to strengthen mineral agent specification

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Cyanosulfidic route well-aligned with literature: glyconitrile from HCN + CH2O is a documented intermediate. Ammonolysis of glyconitrile to aminoacetonitrile is plausible. Literature notes glyconitrile polymerization into amino acid precursors. Environmental flow H→S→B is correct. H2S (mol_006) is listed in molecules but not explicitly used in rxn_008 despite labeling it cyanosulfidic.
suggestions: Clarify the role of H2S in rxn_008 if this is truly cyanosulfidic chemistry; consider adding sulfide as a reactant or photoreductant

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formose route to glycolaldehyde is well-established, and glycolic acid as an intermediate to glycine is supported by literature. However, rxn_012 (glycolic acid amination) is labeled Hydrothermal after Surface reactions, creating an H→S→H environmental regression rather than the preferred H→S→B flow. The amination step itself is less well-documented experimentally for direct glycine formation, and literature notes competitive degradation of glycolic acid.
suggestions: Reassign rxn_012 to Surface or Biochemical environment; specify oxidant for rxn_011 glycolaldehyde oxidation; add mineral catalyst for formose aldol step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longer formose-to-glyoxylate route. Reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine (rxn_014) is well-supported by literature, particularly transamination studies at pH 8 and 50°C. However, same H→S→H environmental regression issue as P4. Two consecutive oxidation steps (rxn_011, rxn_013) without specified oxidants are a concern.
suggestions: Specify oxidant/UV source for glycolic acid to glyoxylate conversion; consider moving rxn_014 to biochemical environment with enzymatic-like mineral catalysis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alternative route via glyconitrile hydrolysis to glycolic acid. The glyconitrile→glycolic acid step is straightforward nitrile hydrolysis. However, glycolic acid amination to glycine (rxn_012 in Hydrothermal) again breaks the preferred environmental flow. Literature supports glycolic acid as a key intermediate but notes competitive degradation.
suggestions: Address environmental regression; specify conditions for rxn_017 hydrolysis more precisely

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longest cyanosulfidic variant through glycolic acid and glyoxylate. Chemically sound with strong literature support for glyoxylate reductive amination. However, 6 steps with environmental regression and two oxidation steps without clear oxidants weaken this pathway.
suggestions: Specify oxidant for glycolic acid to glyoxylate; consider UV photochemistry as oxidant on surface

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydantoin pathway is concise and well-supported. Literature confirms hydantoin formation under hydrothermal conditions and that hydrolysis releases glycine. Environmental flow H→H→B is reasonable (hydantoin forms in hydrothermal, hydrolysis in milder biochemical). Hydantoin is a documented glycine precursor in meteorites. The CO2 and NH3 released in rxn_016 provide nice recycling of feedstocks.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalyst for hydantoin formation; consider whether rxn_015 needs explicit two-step mechanism

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines FTT and formose routes to reach glyoxylate. Chemically plausible but long (6 steps) with same environmental flow issues as P5. Provides useful redundancy through FTT alternative to CO2 fixation.
suggestions: Same as P5; consider consolidating with P5 as a variant entry point

pathway_evaluations.feedback: UV-driven methylamine carboxylation is the weakest pathway. Literature notes sparse data for this route. rxn_019 carboxylation of methylamine by CO2 under UV is not well-established experimentally for glycine production. Some interstellar studies show photochemical methylamine + CO2 reactions but yields and selectivity are poorly characterized. The environmental flow H→H→S is acceptable but the chemistry is speculative.
suggestions: Cite specific experimental evidence for methylamine carboxylation; consider adding a mineral photocatalyst (e.g., TiO2 or ZnS) to make UV-driven step more plausible; this pathway may benefit from replacement with a better-supported route

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-constructed synthesis network that demonstrates strong alignment with prebiotic chemistry literature. The network excels in several areas: (1) Excellent topology with 18 molecules, 20 reactions, 5 hub molecules, and 10 convergent pathways—this is a richly interconnected network. (2) Strong literature alignment—the Strecker, cyanosulfidic, formose, and hydantoin routes are all well-documented in prebiotic chemistry research. Carbon atom tracing is consistent with published isotopic labeling studies. (3) Good use of mineral catalysts in hydrothermal reactions (greigite, magnetite, pyrite). Key issues: (a) Several pathways (P4-P7, P9) exhibit H→S→H environmental regression rather than the preferred H→S→B flow, due to rxn_012 and rxn_014 being assigned to Hydrothermal. (b) The rxn_006/rxn_006_corrected duplication is messy—the original should be removed. (c) H2S (mol_006) is declared as starting material but never consumed by any reaction. (d) HCN (mol_004) is labeled hub_intermediate but has no production reaction—it effectively serves as a starting material. (e) P10's UV-driven carboxylation lacks strong experimental support. (f) Surface reactions in rxn_010, rxn_011, rxn_013, and rxn_017 lack specified mineral catalysts. Despite these issues, 4 of 10 pathways are approved (P1, P2, P3, P8), providing robust redundancy for glycine production. The network successfully demonstrates how simple C1 feedstocks (CO2, CO) are elaborated through hydrothermal mineral catalysis into C1 intermediates that converge through multiple surface chemistry routes to glycine.
